posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
I’ll be there next week and am really looking forward to tasting. I try not to buy into the extreme narratives—whether it’s “vintage of the century” or “disaster vintage”—especially in a region as vast and varied as Bordeaux. It’s rarely so black and white. Even in more challenging years, there are always bright spots if you know where to look, which is where I hope I can offer my folks some first hand insight. Part of the fun is identifying the overachievers and producers who made the most of what the vintage gave them.

For our customers, we’ll definitely be offering the option of European storage. That’ll be included at no cost in the short and medium term, which gives people the flexibility to hold off on shipping and make more informed decisions down the road. I think that kind of flexibility is becoming increasingly important—especially for those building deeper verticals or who place a high value on provenance and long-term condition.

And honestly, for buyers of first growths and other top-tier wines, free storage is a meaningful value-add. Where I have some concern is with more moderately priced wines—where the delta between en primeur pricing (plus eventual shipping and possible tariffs) and wines already on shelves starts to look less compelling. That is what it is, but I’ll still be offering my first hand opinions on wines across the price spectrum.

2024 is the type of vintage Robert Alfret and his friends dream about! The wines are light, quite low in alcohol, and low in pH, so you get a bright, crunchy, red berry oriented wine. In fact, there are several charming, early-drinking wines. And with luck, most will sell for a song. Greetings from Bordeaux…

2024 is not a bad vintage, at its best, there are wines similar in style to 2002, 2008, 2014 etc. At its worst, there are green, over extracted wines which are not very good.

2024 is not a vintage to purchase EP, though the First Growths should be priced below any current vintage on the market.

I have tasted almost every top Left Bank wine. I focus on the Right Bank starting Friday. I have a had few, but not a lot so far.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
For the USA market, with tariffs and no security as to how much those tariffs will be when the wines arrive, it’s a huge concern (that all the producers are aware of,) add to that the loss of the dollar and it’s not pretty.

That being said, First Growths will have significant discounts because they have the most room to reduce prices. You could see releases at 200 Euros ex chateau, meaning prices to consumers should be in the 300 range.

Do not expect the same levels of discounts from most estates. First, it’s much more expensive to produce the challenging vintages than the good years, and yields are lower too, so there’s less wine to sell.

And you need to understand that estates with low prices to start to with, have no room to discount. They’re barely surviving as it is.

Not that EP is always enticing. Regardless of the interest in buying EP, or not, I’m reporting the news, not making it.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Combating disease pressure during the year can require extra help. Sorting is more rigorous and more grapes are left behind. Further sorting in the winery can lead to more grape loss. Less wine is produced, so the fixed costs get spread around a smaller pool of bottles.

That’s just a few.

posts.name: Jim Petrila
posts.cooked: More passes through the vineyard, more sorting, more people, more $

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
JulianD:
Jeff_Leve:

First, it’s much more expensive to produce the challenging vintages than the good years,

Why is that?

Ian gave you a lot of the info. It’s much more labor intensive. For 2024, to combat botrytis and mildew attacks you need teams that are always ready to fight those attacks, which can start at any time, and those diseases can overwhelm a vineyard rapidly.

Some producers had to spray over 20 times this year!

Warm, dry, sunny vintages do not demand as much attention.

Most of that has to do with the quantity of juice that goes into first and second wines. I would be curious to compare percentages of first, second wines in recent vintages and total yields.

Yields range on average 20-32 hectoliters per hectare. Though some estates were in the 40s.

On average, as sorting was intense, the amount placed into the Grand Vin, vs Second wines was close to what each estates normally do on a percentage basis.

First, it’s much more expensive to produce the challenging vintages than the good years,

Why is that?

Ian gave you a lot of the info. It’s much more labor intensive. For 2024, to combat botrytis and mildew attacks you need teams that are always ready to fight those attacks, which can start at any time, and those diseases can overwhelm a vineyard rapidly.

Some producers had to spray over 20 times this year!

Warm, dry, sunny vintages do not demand as much attention.

Very good point! I shudder to think of the chemical residues that some wines will contain. I remember a study made of 2002s that revealed some quite alarming figures.

There are strict limits as to how much copper can be used. Today, vineyards are limited to 28 KG per hectare over a 7 year period.

Vineyards today are much cleaner than they were in 2002, and a world apart from the chemicals used in the 70s, 80s, and 90s. You find more certified organic vineyards ever year.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Interesting synopsis by Farr

Browser

2024 Bordeaux - notes from the Chairman

2024 is my 42nd Bordeaux en primeur campaign and it’s certainly one of the most challenging vintages that I have seen in my career in the wine trade.

Before we get on to the vintage itself, let’s talk about the market. Because it is undeniable that, more than any other factor, Bordeaux en primeur tends to be priced by market conditions at the time of release. Well, quite simply, the market is terrible. Unless proprietors want to put their heads in the sand (it’s happened before, and maybe some will again?) we are facing some hugely negative issues in the Bordeaux wine trade.

Prices of immature Bordeaux wines have been falling for the past two years.

Most Bordeaux negociants are over-stocked and paying interest to the banks while the value of their inventories declines. They are not in a position to finance the vintage.

Most UK merchants are losing money. They are not in a position to buy for stock.

The Chinese market for fine wine has virtually disappeared. They will not be buying.

The “Trump Tariffs” mean that US merchants have no idea what taxes they will have to pay when the wines are available for delivery, so are unlikely to be buying.

All these factors mean one thing. The Bordeaux trade really needs these wines to sell. This vintage is going to have to be extremely well priced when it is released. If it isn’t, the châteaux will have to keep it.

I fully expect that most wines will be released at lower prices than those at which the consumer can buy the same château in any other vintage. We should see good classed growths for sale from not much more than £20 per bottle. Second growths at under £50 per bottle and the price of some first growths (for the first time in a decade) starting with a “2” per bottle. So, given that prices are going to be low, what about the vintage?

Well, it was a very tricky year for the winemakers of Bordeaux. If you sat back and let nature take its course, you had serious problems in the vineyard. And, if you made wine from all the grapes that you picked, then you had something as potentially poor as 2013. The amazing thing, however, is that some châteaux rose to the challenge and made some very attractive wine that will make for good early drinking.

First of all, it was essential to treat the vineyards (properties such as Domaine de Chevalier decided to abandon their “organic” principles for a year in order to save the harvest). Secondly, you to had to make massive selection decisions. If there is a buzz word for Bordeaux 2024, then it is “sorting”.

Under-ripe and over-ripe fruit had to be rejected by pickers in the vineyard (we heard stories of plots taking three times as long to harvest as normal). Further sorting had to take place before the fruit was sent to the wineries. Once received in the chai, the fruit had to be sorted manually, often again by optical sorting machines and, for those who could afford them, the latest technology to be employed was densimetric sorting machines that eliminated berries that didn’t reach the required ripeness levels.

Most winemakers then had to add sugar (chaptalisation) to beef up the concentration of their wine, while others used bleeding (saignée) or reverse osmosis to remove water from the musts. Further selection meant that many growers produced more second wine than first wine and that less good vats were often sold off in bulk. Some châteaux have produced a fraction of a normal crop in order to maintain quality. Normally this is a recipe for higher prices – not this year!

We heard many stories of how the final yields – the amount of grape juice actually used to make the wines – were as little as half the amount that was actually produced by the vines in the vineyard.

The silver lining this year is the excellent quality of the dry white wines and Sauternes. After Haut Brion Blanc our Master of Wine, Thomas Parker, selects Domaine de Chevalier Blanc as his wine of the vintage.

But if you like to purchase a few cases of claret every year en primeur, don’t despair. All the sacrifices and expense incurred by the best producers to be successful in 2024 have resulted in some very decent red wines and – if the prices turn out to be as low as we hope - then there will be wines to buy. This is my “top ten” pick of wines from across the region at a range of price points.

L’Eglise Clinet – this simply does not look like, smell like or taste like a 2024 Bordeaux. The finest wine that we tasted on the right bank this year. They even had a normal yield and alcohol level. Unreal for the vintage. (Estimate £1,800–£2,250 per dozen)

Montrose – if any wine tastes like a first growth then this is it. In fact, I think that this is better than the first growths this year. Huge efforts were made here to make the best quality possible from only the heart of the estate. Just one third of the crop is in this “grand vin”. (Estimate £1,000–£1,280 per dozen)

Canon – in the same way that Montrose is of first growth quality on the left bank, then so is this on the right bank. The neighbours at Figeac, Pavie, Angélus and Ausone may match this for scores (or maybe not?), but Canon trumps them on price. (Estimate £780-£960 per dozen)

Les Carmes Haut Brion – a genuine contender for wine of the vintage in recent years and in 2024 they may well have done it. A very special early-ripening vineyard. Exhaustive sorting processes, inspired, unique and downright brilliant winemaking. (Estimate £680-£850 per dozen)

Lynch Bages – powerful and classically Pauillac in style. Incredible depth for the vintage and we expect this to be the lowest priced vintage in the market for this benchmark claret. (Estimate £600-£760 per dozen)

Rauzan Ségla – there are three great estates at the heart of Margaux – Château Margaux, Château Palmer and this one. We couldn’t decide which we liked the best, but the price here makes Rauzan the “no-brainer”. (Estimate £520-£650 per dozen)

Léoville Barton – proper Saint Julien. This château is always generously priced on release, and we could see a sub £50 a bottle “super-second growth” here. (Estimate £500–£620 per dozen)

Domaine de Chevalier – as the prices at neighbours Smith Haut Lafitte and Haut Bailly drift away, we were once again reminded of the consistent great quality here - and the Bernards have promised us that it will be very reasonably priced. (Estimate £400-£500 per dozen)

Les Cruzelles – this tastes like a Pomerol in a very good vintage. A resounding success for 2024 and you simply cannot buy a better right bank wine for the price. 13.6% ABV and 100% natural sugars. (Estimate £150-£185 per dozen)

Montlandrie – no apologies for another Durantou wine in the list. Dark and serious with an incredible quality to price ratio. It is a simple fact that there is no better wine in Bordeaux at a lower price than this. (Estimate £125–£155 per dozen)

The en primeur section of our website is now live and you can read our full report on the vintage, written by Thomas Parker MW. You can find a full, detailed introduction, tasting notes and recommendations on what to buy. Notes and scores from the critics will be added as and when they are published. Please also read the section on how to order.

Stephen Browett

Crunch Time For Bordeaux En Primeur​

[/td]
[td]
We are on the cusp of the 2014 Bordeaux En Primeur campaign and it’s fair to say no one is looking forward to it. The poor vintage aside, certain headwinds will make the next weeks very challenging. The market is in its third year of price declines, with recent vintages trading in the secondary market below the Chateau’s offered price to Negociants (so-called Ex Chateau Price). No one recalls this phenomenon happening before.

In the supply chain from Bordeaux Negoce to European and London merchants, there will not be much buying for stock, if any at all. The Asian market has been AWOL for years and now the Trump Tariffs have killed the strongest market for Bordeaux wines. The uncertainty of taxation rates for importing wines into the US in the next few years is a massive hindrance to participation in any En Primeur campaign (see previous note HERE).

Finally the most important lesson is not to listen to any wine critic as part of the wine buying calculus. The wine critic’s role is to provide a quantitative judgement but those voices have far too often harmonised into universal praise, rather than any critical analysis. The economics of Bordeaux En Primeur make it almost impossible for any wine critic to offer anything other than positives. Unless their recommendations provide convincing arguments of comparative value against wines current trading in the market, disregard them completely as part of the wine buying decision making process.

From an investment perspective, poor quality vintages can provide outsized returns. 2007 and 2013 stand out with over 50% and nearly 40% total returns respectively to the retail release price (Ex-London Price). However, recent poorer vintages such as 2017 and 2021 have significant double digit negative returns. This highlights a shift to a more aggressive pricing strategy from the Chateau’s who have also sought to hold back supply at initial release to boost prices. And herein lies the fundamental problem with Bordeaux En Primeur- the greed of the producers.

They are businesses, with most of the top Chateau being part of large conglomerates. Like any business, they aim to extract as much returns from their investments with their pricing strategy. They employ PR and Marketing People (including collaborations with wine critics) to convince consumers to pay increasingly high prices for their wines each year. However, wine is a commodity. Previous vintages are traded activity on the secondary market, which is subject to comparative analysis; in other words a Wine Curve. Under and overpriced vintages for each wine can easily be identified. This means when producers ask for premiums or even the same value for their En Primeur wines against similar rated vintages- this doesn’t make any sense. As been mentioned previously, En Primeur is not about buying wine, its about speculating on the price of wine in the future.

Given the scale of wealth destruction that participation in Bordeaux En Primeur has come to represent, the Chateau’s pricing strategy must change. Continuing the trend of failed campaigns will only further undermine its brand, festering an increasing cynicism around Bordeaux En Primeur which will cannibalise their marketplace.

Ian_A:

Finally the most important lesson is not to listen to any wine critic as part of the wine buying calculus.

Agreed. I did not buy 2021 EP and did a little research on winesearcher before trying a few (looking at sites like Zachy’s and K&L which tend to have multiple reviewers), and what I read was terrifyingly disconnected from what was in the glass. Not a bad vintage, but not priced properly at all- however the never-ending enthusiasm of some critics would have one believing it was far better than it ever could be.

It has always been difficult, especially in Burgundy, for a critic to be brutally honest every year and remain in good graces with everyone over time. In these days and times I think it must be nearly impossible, and certainly for a lot of the newer generation of critics who did not come into the game having already made their money in another profession thus giving them personal access and a bit of general insulation that others might not have.

There are still some very respectable voices about, and the most clever are able to not be too disparaging while also using certain words and terms that will resonate with certain audiences, but score inflation is a very real thing and these days a great many critics are basically cheerleaders for the trade- not that this is a new thing…

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
If the WA scores for Bordeaux 2024 are a come back to reasonable wine critic we should applaud it.

I tasted the Union Grand Cru portfolio of the last vintages in Zurich and scratched my head when I saw the scores for 2021 wines in bottle. Often only a 2 or 3 point difference to superb vintages such as 2019 and 2020. So the 100 point rating system had become more of a 10 point system. Why?

One reason is that wines with a lower score than 90 are hard to sell. But the goal of wine critics is not to sell wine but inform the consumer in a reliable manner. The initial scoring system said more or less: 80 to 85 = good. 85 to 90 = very good. 90 to 95 = outstanding. 95 to 100 = extraordinary. I think it would be beneficial and more informative for the consumer to have a wider scoring range.

In some vintages mother nature makes it impossible to produce something extraordinary. That does not mean those wines are not worth to be bought and drunk. They are often accessible earlier and a very good companion at the dinner table. This is the message for us consumers. The message for the Chateaux is that they should price the wines according to their real quality.

Forecasts say that the best 2024 Bordeaux can be had to reasonable prices. If this is true it would be the right direction.

posts.name: THOMAS MAUSS
posts.cooked:
In my opinion, the scores recently published by The Wine Advocate raise new concerns about the publication. If the tasters intended to make a statement by awarding widely varying scores to indicate that this vintage is not exceptional, it nonetheless results, in my view, in significant inconsistencies in the way wines are rated on the website.

I’ve been tasting en primeur wines since the 2009 vintage, and having sampled several hundred 2024s, I find the scores to be quite harsh. Perhaps I’m not entirely objective either, given my involvement in the trade.

Recently, I tasted a wine rated 95+ by Luis Gutiérrez — Envínate La Santa de Úrsula 2021. It’s an interesting wine with a distinctive profile, but equally marked by brettanomyces and flavors of old barrels that likely weren’t very clean. If I take The Wine Advocate at face value, only four Bordeaux wines from 2024 could hope to reach a higher level than this Envínate. I find that utterly absurd.

Finally, I find that giving a score such as 90–93 — meaning the wine will likely end up at 90, 91, 92, or 93 — is not a strong or precise commitment from a critic.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hi Thomas,

I think already we are trying to be consistent within Bordeaux over the last decade, without getting into comparing Bordeaux and the Canaries! The scores reflect that we think this is overall the weakest vintage since 2013; though it is quite a bit better than 2013, and of course there are some estates who have, against the odds, made wines that are likely to turn out to match other more favorable vintages. People of course tend to reduce a report to either favorable or negative (you will see above some criticism of us for being too favorable on 2021, for example), but the reality if you look wine by wine, and set the 2024 reviews in the context of the last decade of scores, is that it is quite a bit more nuanced than that.

As for bracketed scores, that has always been the practice at TWA, and the brackets may expand or contract to reflect our level of confidence. Between rather fragile samples this year that could change a lot from day to day, even at the estate, and changeable weather during some of the tastings which never helps wines show their best, there were some instances where we gave a broader bracket. But this is the case ever year, and was the practice of my predecessors back to RP, so nothing new. I do not think false precision for precision’s sake is in anyone’s interest during EP.

Best,

William

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think the big problem the region has is that, from the producer perspective, the wines are really quite reasonably priced and good value. You can get excellent terroir, great pedigree (if that counts for you), and high-quality winemaking for less, in greater volume, in Bordeaux than most other places. I think the Bordelais are right to point this out and be proud of it.

But from the consumer perspective, one looks at the en primeur price and then at the market price of other vintages and the value proposition looks much less appealing (you can see people doing this very explicitly in this thread). By virtue of the volumes produced and the commercial system that distributes the wines, Bordeaux seems to have been “commoditized” to a much greater degree than many other wines, and so people think about the price of new vintages in a way they don’t seem to with other wines. People buy e.g. their Gonon allocation and would be happy to pay the same price every year, irrespective of vintage quality; but that is not how many consumers who follow the wines think about Bordeaux. It is a problem that’s easier to diagnose than to solve for the region I suspect…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Scores are down a lot compared to recent vintages, but “terrible” is a simplification. As ever we try to call it how we see it, and some of the reviews certainly are very critical. We are also very clear about how we situate the vintage vis a vis other recent vintages. But if you look back at RP’s 2001 EP scores, for example, you see Mouton at 90-92, Pétrus at 91-93, Lafite at 92-94, Pichon Comtesse at 89-91 etc etc and I don’t think anyone today would claim that 2001 was a failed vintage.

Sadly the quality of the wines is likely to get lost in the discussion around EP. In Bordeaux one often hears that en primeur is some sort of amazing opportunity, as it brings all the world to Bordeaux and makes all the world talk about Bordeaux. If the result, however, is that all the world simply talks about price, I am not sure it is doing the region’s wines all that much of a service…

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Thomas_Mauss:

my opinion, the scores recently published by The Wine Advocate raise new concerns about the publication. If the tasters intended to make a statement by awarding widely varying scores to indicate that this vintage is not exceptional, it nonetheless results, in my view, in significant inconsistencies in the way wines are rated on the website.

I’ve been tasting en primeur wines since the 2009 vintage, and having sampled several hundred 2024s, I find the scores to be quite harsh. Perhaps I’m not entirely objective either, given my involvement in the trade.

Recently, I tasted a wine rated 95+ by Luis Gutiérrez — Envínate La Santa de Úrsula 2021. It’s an interesting wine with a distinctive profile, but equally marked by brettanomyces and flavors of old barrels that likely weren’t very clean. If I take The Wine Advocate at face value, only four Bordeaux wines from 2024 could hope to reach a higher level than this Envínate. I find that utterly absurd.

Finally, I find that giving a score such as 90–93 — meaning the wine will likely end up at 90, 91, 92, or 93 — is not a strong or precise commitment from a critic.

I look for consistency within the reviews. It’s a little unfair to inject a second reviewer, Gutierrez, then yourself and expect the results to make any sense. I have a hard enough time finding sense and consistency from single reviewers.

My own feeling, after several tastings of recent vintages, is not that 2024 were underscored, it’s that the others have been overscored.

I have not tasted 2024, but it sounds as if this is not another sunny vintage, and the grapes struggled to ripen. A good deal of chaptalization took place. This is not the paradigm the critics are used to, and perhaps the negativity comes from it. But to be fair, the scores are in line with those from average or lesser vintages in the era before climate change.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s quite complex so I’ll expand… On the one hand, less clean and less completely and homogeneously ripe fruit produces wines that are more fragile to oxidation. On the other, many châteaux also like to show samples that were drawn the day before, or two days before, because if not the samples can also reduce in the sample bottle—but obviously this can backfire, too. On top of that, 2024, perhaps due to residues of phytosanitary products from harvest or maybe just the less clean quality of fruit, could also be quite reductive. So as opposed to 2022 samples which were quite robust and could be kicked around, we encountered quite a few 2024 samples that were a bit tired, and also quite a few that were notably reduced. At least some châteaux told us they upped the SO2 levels in the samples to protect them; but that can in turn suppress fruit and foreground tannin: and we did taste some samples that seemed muted by quite high free SO2. Add to this the more delicate structural matrices of the wines, which could show their new oak quite a bit on some days, and not at all on others, and I think you get a sense of why tasting EP is quite a demanding technical exercise, irrespective of any aesthetic judgements.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Malolactic in barrel certainly gives a different profile, especially early in élevage, and it is something one tries to take into account. But I wouldn’t say it makes wines more or less fragile. As ever, it’s an ostensibly simple technique that conceals many variables: the strain of malolactic bacteria (some make more diacetyl than others), the temperature of MLF (higher = more diacetyl), etc, some of which are arguably actually more important than the vessel in which MLF takes place.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Malolactic in barrel certainly gives a different profile, especially early in élevage, and it is something one tries to take into account. But I wouldn’t say it makes wines more or less fragile. As ever, it’s an ostensibly simple technique that conceals many variables: the strain of malolactic bacteria (some make more diacetyl than others), the temperature of MLF (higher = more diacetyl), etc, some of which are arguably actually more important than the vessel in which MLF takes place.

My apologies, I was a little unclear. I don’t think malo in barrel makes the wines more fragile, but in 2024, the wines were less “robust” to begin with, and the malo in barrel may have reacted differently to less large scaled wines. Given that at this stage,the wines are not fully formed, I am wondering if because of this, and because the wines are tasted pretty soon after the malo is completed, whether it had greater impact on these less hefty wines.

It’s pure speculation, with no possible proof, unless some winemakers experimented with two different regimes.

That’s fair but the risk is 2+ years away. There will likely be a new trade deal by then. The greater risk are wines that are in bottle and need to be imported now.

EP 24 issues are with vintage quality and pricing with better vintages available to buy now.

I think it is wrong to think that risk that is 2+ years away is automatically lower than near-term risk. The number of factors that play into risk over time don’t cancel each other out the longer the duration of time . We live in a world now in which the top official of the US rug-pulled every trade deal the US had in the world in part because of the way he felt and based on trade deficit math that doesn’t help anyone. I’d say there is risk now, and there is even greater risk for the wines that are 2+ years away. That makes the current campaign all of the aforementioned vintage quality and pricing issues, compounded by what I consider to be higher risk of increased tariffs, taxes, etc., on alcohol/wine in the future. War, French government changes, US government changes, currency shifts, non-US/non-Euro trade issues that bleed over, and even dismantling the En Primeur system all play into the risk, and all of those things are increasing in risk IMO rather than decreasing.

posts.cooked: Yeah, I think there were two hurdles to cross: getting through the mildew with a functional canopy and crop intact; and then getting the grapes ripe. posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
IMO the best thing about wine is its diversity. And I love to follow the evolution of wines during their life.

What I do not like that much are generalizations. Not every Bordeaux or Burgundy gets better with age. This depends on the vintage and wine making style. And the formula the older the better is misleading based on my experience as a wine lover for more than 40 years.

I dislike oxidation but I learned that many people are more tolerant to it than me. If a Barolo develops a lot of aceto balsamico aromatics and a Bordeaux gets into the direction of Madeira it is to late for me to enjoy the wines qualities. And while I agree that some Rieslings are fine at age 30/40 this is not always the rule IMO. If a Riesling has lost its freshness the best days are gone IMO.

posts.cooked: I certainly think they will be approachable young (in instances where the grapes were tolerably ripe and extraction gentle), even for some of the more naturally powerful wines of the vintage such as Carmes and Pontet Canet. Obviously they won’t be showing tertiary characteristics, but you should have a lot of aromatic range and complexity given the profile of the wines.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
EberhardSchneider:

For me, the wine of the vintage with very high ratings from all tasters is Les Carmes Haut Brion

I’ve been trying to wrap my head around the praise for LCHB. To me, this is a case of winemaking philosophy taking precedence over what the vintage actually gave.

The stems just didn’t seem lignified enough in 2024 to justify the amount used in the grand vin—and while I’ve never cared much for their infusion-style approach, I can at least understand that others do. Still, it felt like a mismatch here. The second wine, with less whole cluster, showed more clarity and drinkability for me.

I respect the ambition to define a house style (even if I don’t personally care for it stylistically), but in this case, it feels like they chased typicity at the expense of balance. Curious to see how it evolves, but at this stage, it didn’t come together for me.

Brian_G_r_a_f_s_t_r_o_m:

Received an offer for 1986 Leoville Barton at $160 (iirc) last week.

That example helps illuminate my epic apathy over this 2024 EP campaign.

How is this apples to apples? Old Bordeaux has been a bargain for a long time – certainly well before the EP24 campaign.

As a straight math problem, except in the case of the very rare wines that outpace the market, it’s likely to be the best financial decision to cellar very little and just buy mature wine on the secondary market when you want to drink it. If you invested $20 in 1986 and got 8% returns, you’d have over $400 today. So you could buy the wine that originally cost $20 and have $200+ left over.

However, for many folks collecting is part of the fun, following the wine over the course of many years is very interesting, and you’ll always have 100% knowledge of how the wine was treated since release.

JamesG:

They must be using the Pontet Canet pricing algorithm

HenryB:

Palmer’s pric isnt even remotely interesting, imho, when 2019 is widely available at a comparable price.

Ex-cellar, it’s -33% vs 2023, whereas PC was like -7% or something. PC completely blew it and could’ve been the success of the vintage for US buyers if they did comparable price cuts to others in the 25-35% range.

The prevailing price on 2019 Palmer is ~$299, but it looks like there are maybe some retailers that still have stock from en primeur cost basis and are competitive with what EP price on 2024 will be. I like both 2019 and 2024 Palmer (one of my favorite wines personally that I tasted). Relative to their peers, I think it’s a pretty easy case to make that Palmer actually did better in 2024 than they did in 2019. Whether the wine itself is better in 2024 vs 2019 will be a matter of taste.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
HoosJustinG:
Brian_G_r_a_f_s_t_r_o_m:

Received an offer for 1986 Leoville Barton at $160 (iirc) last week.

That example helps illuminate my epic apathy over this 2024 EP campaign.

How is this apples to apples? Old Bordeaux has been a bargain for a long time – certainly well before the EP24 campaign.

As a straight math problem, except in the case of the very rare wines that outpace the market, it’s likely to be the best financial decision to cellar very little and just buy mature wine on the secondary market when you want to drink it. If you invested $20 in 1986 and got 8% returns, you’d have over $400 today. So you could buy the wine that originally cost $20 and have $200+ left over.

However, for many folks collecting is part of the fun, following the wine over the course of many years is very interesting, and you’ll always have 100% knowledge of how the wine was treated since release.

Well, yeah. I’m not going to argue any of that. I just think it’s important for us (me) to sometimes receive this reminder that other options are available on the market, particularly as one is faced with the temptation of buying the new-on-the-market vintage, particularly when that vintage is apparently so poor.

Yes, collecting is part of the fun, and following a wine over the course of many years is fun and very interesting, and yes there’s a lot to be said for known provenance, particularly when it is yourself who has been carefully taking care of the wines over the course of their lifetimes. But it’s also okay to mix-in different buying/cellaring strategies, too — one’s wine collection/consumption need not be monochromatic in these regards, and I’d suggest there’s a lot of benefit in having varied buying/consumption approaches. So, as we look at what appears to be a very weak 2024 vintage being offered EP, coupled with a wildly unpredictable tariffs situation, this EP campaign feels like a time when one might be best advised to mix-in some kind of buying/consumption other than “buy EP.”

So, back to my example: if I have $160 to spend on Bordeaux right now, and I’m choosing between 2024 EP and other stuff currently on the market, I see the $160 much better spent on that '86 Barton than on some 2024 EP bottle(s). YMMV.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
If I were young enough to be buying young Bordeaux, my decision whether to buy EP would be guided by:

It’s not a great vintage but there are likely to be some excellent wines if I choose carefully based on my known preferences and trusted critics.

Might be worth buying EP if those wines also happen to be significantly cheaper than any of the release prices since 2019. The last time I bought EP was 2019. It was because the prices were good enough to entice me out of retirement from buying young Bordeaux (I’d quit with 2016).

Weigh the potential for a good deal against the risk associated with buying on someone else’s barrel notes without tasting yourself, and maybe getting a dud.

Ignore the tariff risk when making the buy/pass decision today, except when choosing a vendor. Make sure you won’t be hit with restocking charges if you cancel. If tariffs are in effect when the wines are released and there are no attractive options for waiting them out, cancel the order. Tariffs were a concern with the 2019s. While they never materialized, all of my vendors said they would offer the option to cancel at no cost.

Unless you’re after something highly allocated or need 375s or large formats, availability shouldn’t factor into the decision. I would not be prodded into buying for fear that the wines won’t be available on release. It’s possible that most will be sold through French supermarkets like the ‘91s and ‘92s, but I think that unlikely. This vintage isn’t that bad. As Craig_G pointed out above, plenty of ’17s and ‘21s made it over here.

Thank you for the 2014 link, Jeff. I’ll peruse that later with a glass in my hand.

On chaptalization not being secretive. Respectfully, I’m calling b.s. on that one. I just spent some time trying to find that information for 2021, 2023, and 2024 online. I was only able to find bits and pieces; certainly no 2021/23/24 Bordeaux Chaptalization report, or something of that kind.

The authors of this article about 2021 Bordeaux said, “Producers can seem keen to gloss over or shy away from discussing the tricks of their trade. … We asked about the potentially contentious topic at every property we visited, receiving only a small handful of slightly wary responses on the topic (“We did what we had to do,” one winemaker said, with a smile).” That doesn’t sound like producers are being terribly forthcoming on the topic.

Some producers were somewhat more forthcoming:
" At [Ch. Cheval Blanc Pierre-Olivier Clouet firmly stated, “We never touch the natural balance of the wine.” That said, they did chaptalise three plots in the end, but none of them made it into the Grand Vin. Delphine Kolasa of Margaux’s Marquis d’Alesme believes that you can almost always feel a warmness from the additional, unnatural alcohol – although she noted a few properties who had managed it so well you could barely tell.

At Les Carmes Haut-Brion, Guillaume Pouthier highlighted the balance needed with the year’s acidity levels. The relative lack of sunshine resulted in grapes high in malic acid, meaning that the malolactic fermentation (where hard, green-apple malic acid is converted into softer lactic acid) had a greater impact in the wines, reducing the overall acidity. With this in mind, he felt that chaptalisation could lead to wines feeling “flat” – but it wasn’t a necessity for him, given he managed to reach 13.5% natural alcohol thanks to the property’s warmer, suburban microclimate. …

*But there were plenty of outstanding estates which felt it was an essential element to success in the year. At Le Pin, Jacques Thienpont remarked how they had to employ “forgotten methods”, noting that it was more than 20 years since he’d had cause to chaptalise in Pomerol. He used it on a couple of vats. *

Joséphine Duffau-Lagarrosse– the young vigneronne who has taken over her family’s estate – joked about having to learn how to do it, so alien is the process to a millennial winemaker.

Similarly, Christian Moueix – another of the Right Bank’s old guard – noted how he used it almost every year in the 1970s, but hadn’t needed to for a long time. For him the key was not to overdo it, enough to add a maximum 1% alcohol. There was a clear consensus on this among proponents – adding half or one degree (of alcohol) was beneficial, but if you pushed things too far, you’d lose balance in the wines.

https://www.frw.co.uk/editorial/chaptalisation-a-spoonful-of-sugar

I could keep searching and post many more similar comments/passages from other articles. While conducting my fruitless search for this non-secretive, yet oh-so-elusive Bordeaux Chaptalization report, I learned the practice has been banned in Bordeaux since 2019, and can now only be done by and through an application and approval process. Why ban something if it’s not “bad”?

I’m sorry, Jeff, but you simply saying this information is not secretive does not make it so. If you can find this report/information, please direct our attention to it — I am genuinely curious to know the facts. Even many of the “forthcoming” producers did not apparently divulge detailed information about their chaptalization usage.

And as for people asking you about blends, alc. levels, and readiness to drink — yeah, sure – those things are important, too. But interest in those aspects does not mean chaptalization information is unimportant or that there is a lack of interest about it. Some of the passages I quoted, above, mention the impact that chaptalization can have on the finished wines, both good and bad; that sounds like something important to me. YMMV.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Like everything else in winemaking, there is an art to chaptalization, and I have never figured what is the right way. I have heard different theories and practices. As it is less common than it used to be, some winemakers have had to learn from scratch, and the more they have to do it in any vintage, the more critical it is to get it right. I have tasted wines where it is pretty obvious something went wrong, and that is usually found in the finish, when flavors stop abruptly, but the structural elements remain.

To Jeff’s point that we haven’t tasted the wines, it seems to be that much more critical to know what has been added to the must. Many of the critics who go to Bordeaux, taste, score and sell their opinions are probably less experienced than some of the winemakers, who at least have an older generation to turn to, and ask the questions. I have never been particularly comfortable with some of the people scoring, and here they are dealing with stuff they have never had to before.

So yes, if the scores are to make any sense at all, questions about if and how much wines underwent chaptalization are totally relevant.

If the information is reported to the appropriate authorities, but not made available to the consumer, then from a consumer’s perspective that information is being held in secret. It would appear, based on some published articles, that when wine publications come asking about it, they often get non-specific “answer” answers. “Holding something in secret” does not mean that information doesn’t exist, or that it has not been disseminated to any audience whatsoever; rather, it means there is some audience(s) from whom the information is kept. Some wineries post tech sheets online, in emails, or in physical marketing materials. I have never seen one include information about the use of chaptalization, or Mega Purple, or acid additions, or or or …. It is quite clear that wineries generally do not want to divulge this information, and they don’t, except to the extent they must disclose to certain government agencies.

Furthermore, if acquiring this information requires going from producer to producer, and asking the winemaker directly, then that simply is impractical for nearly every consumer, and — given access constraints — likely impossible for all but the privileged few. I am respectfully calling b.s. on the contention that wineries are not secretive about chaptalization, including Bdx specifically. Next time I go wine tasting in Bdx (I’ve only had the privilege once in my life), I’ll ask directly about chaptalization and see what kinds of answers I get. I’m betting I’ll get very few producers saying, “Yes, we chaptalized this much in this particular vintage.” Nonetheless, I will go with a willingness to be surprised and proven wrong.

Again, this is one point on which wine critics could be helpful to their audiences.

I am sorry but it’s not obvious to me. With 24, you should consider that most producers with Cabernet needed chaptalization to varying degrees and percentages.

A bit of history for you, chaptalization was discovered in the early 1800s. I’m not sure when it became wide spread, but as a guess, it was used in the 19th and 20th century, especially in cooler vintages.

It was legalized in Bordeaux in 1951. I’m betting it was used in some degree in a lot of vintages. Even on the top years. As to how much, it would depend on the vintage. But as it was cooler in those days, and the search for phenolic ripeness was not equal to today, I’m guessing it was used to some degree in almost every vintage.

Keep in mind, until recently, Bordeaux was historically not high in alcohol. Climatic conditions and consumer taste are quite different than they were just a few decades ago.

Today, the practice is highly regulated by the INAO. It’s on a vintage by vintage basis and producers must ask before chaptalization.

There are very heavy fines for not following the rules. I’ve forgotten the specifics on the rules. But I’ll try asking tomorrow.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Something else to keep in mind about chaptalization, now that you are aware that it is much more widespread than you had previously considered.

It’s not a simple one-size-fits all, so that even if I thought the stats were interesting to enough people, (which I don’t) it’s a lot of work. Too much work for me.

For example, only some parcels or select vats are chaptalized, others are not. Every parcel or vat does not have the same level of chaptalization, they vary widely from .5 to 1.5 degrees.

Some chaptalized lots enter the Grand Vin, but my guess is more enter the second and third wines.

However. it gets more complicated. This is what takes place. Each appellation petitions the INAO for chaptalization. In 2024, it was easy, every appellation was allowed a maximum of 1.5 degrees. This changes every vintage meaning that in some years, select appellations are allowed for example, .5. while others are permitted up to 1.5 degrees.

From there, after each appellation has submitted a request to the INAO, wineries must declare their first enrichment 48 hours before doing so. After that, any additional enrichments are recorded in a specific notebook that is provided to customs.

The only thing sugar coated here are some lots of wine. I’m hoping this answers all your questions regarding chaptalization, and why I personally only cover it in my opening vintage report, and not on a vineyard, by vineyard basis.

What does help me is the idea that it’s like adding a bit of salt to food. I love cooking — do it nearly every day — and am keenly aware not only of salt’s utility, but also its necessity; I don’t look down upon food that has been appropriately salted. I’m trying to get myself to the same place with chaptalization — if producers were more forthcoming about the extent to which their wines have been chaptalized that would help a lot.

If it’s too much work for you, or any reviewer, to include in their reporting both (1) if a wine has been chaptalized, and (2) the extent to which the finished wine has been chaptalized, then that’s fine, but it doesn’t change the fact that said reporting is then deficient in this regard, and is missing an opportunity to be helpful to certain audience members in this regard. Obviously, this is up to the reviewer to decide if it’s “worth their time and effort.”

I don’t care if only some parcels are chaptalized and others are not. All I care about is the extent to which the finished wine is chaptalized, as that’s what matters in the end.

I don’t care about the petition/application process. Again, all I care about is the extent to which the finished wine is chaptalized.

And I don’t care if chaptalization information is shared with the government — I care about whether it is being shared with me. If the government knows the information I want to know, but I don’t, then I am still without the information.

No, all of my questions regarding chaptalization have not been answered. I have been quite clear on exactly what information I am looking for: I want to know the extent to which the finished wine has been chaptalized.

I’m not particularly a betting man with wine, nor are most people here, but if I was, I would take 10:1 odds that in five years time we don’t look back at 2024 as an amazing, once a decade buying opportunity.

Just my view. As an amateur with more than a decade of en primeur.

That’s not withstanding ones own stylistic preferences, obviously. I don’t mind when my wine drops in price, either, I just get a bit pissed off when it costs more than £400 for a case of 6 that then drops in price and is widely available down the line.

I know I sound like a Bordeaux hater but it’s actually the majority of my cellar and I think it’s a shame it has such a commercial, commodity lens on it, but at the same time I think one crafts their own fate…
